content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 29 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 227–228 | **Source Images:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/DEU_029_page_001.jpg, DEU_029_page_002.jpg | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 161–162 — DEU_029_vaticanus_page_001.jpg, DEU_029_vaticanus_page_002.jpg

---

*[Note: Hebrew versification has ch. 29 beginning at v.1 with the summary statement about the Horev covenant, while English versions commonly treat this as the end of ch. 28. EMET follows the MT Hebrew chapter division: 29:1 = "These are the words of the covenant..." (English 28:69), with the chapter continuing through what English versions number 29:1–28.]*

**1** These are the words of the covenant that the LORD commanded Moshe to make with the sons of Yisra'el in the land of Moav, in addition to the covenant that he made with them at Chorev.

**2** And Moshe called to all Yisra'el and said to them: You have seen all that the LORD did before your eyes in the land of Mitsrayim — to Pharaoh and to all his servants and to all his land —

**3** the great trials that your eyes saw, the signs and those great wonders.

**4** Yet the LORD has not given you a heart to understand, or eyes to see, or ears to hear, until this day.

**5** And I led you forty years in the wilderness; your garments did not wear out on you, and your sandal did not wear out on your foot.

**6** Bread you did not eat, and wine and strong drink you did not drink — so that you might know that I am the LORD your God.

**7** And when you came to this place, Sichon the king of Cheshbon and 'Og the king of Bashan came out to meet us for battle, and we struck them.

**8** And we took their land and gave it as an inheritance to Re'uven and to Gad and to the half tribe of Menasheh.

**9** And you shall keep the words of this covenant and do them, so that you may act wisely [taskilу] in all that you do.

**10** You are standing today, all of you, before the LORD your God — your chiefs, your tribes, your elders, your officers, every man of Yisra'el,

**11** your little ones, your wives, and your sojourner who is within your camps, from the hewer of your wood to the drawer of your water —

**12** to enter into the covenant of the LORD your God and into his oath [alah] that the LORD your God is making with you today,

**13** in order to establish you today as his people, and that he may be your God, as he spoke to you and as he swore to your fathers — to Avraham, to Yitschak, and to Ya'akov.

**14** And not with you alone am I making this covenant and this oath,

**15** but with whoever is standing here with us today before the LORD our God, and also with whoever is not here with us today —

**16** for you know how we dwelt in the land of Mitsrayim, and how we passed through the midst of the nations through whom you passed;

**17** and you saw their detestable things and their idols — wood and stone, silver and gold — which were among them.

**18** Lest there be among you a man or woman or family or tribe whose heart is turning away today from the LORD our God, to go and serve the gods of those nations — lest there be among you a root bearing poisonous and bitter fruit [rosh ve-la'anah] —

**19** and it comes to pass, when he hears the words of this oath [alah], that he blesses himself in his heart, saying: I shall have peace, though I walk in the stubbornness of my heart — in order to add the watered [to] the thirsty [one] —

**20** the LORD will not be willing to forgive him; rather, the anger of the LORD and his jealousy shall smoke against that man, and all the curse [alah] written in this scroll shall lie upon him, and the LORD will blot out his name from under the heavens.

**21** And the LORD will single him out from all the tribes of Yisra'el for disaster, according to all the curses of the covenant written in this scroll of the Torah.

**22** And the next generation, your sons who rise up after you, and the foreigner who comes from a distant land, shall say when they see the plagues of that land and the sicknesses with which the LORD has made it sick —

**23** [that] all its soil [is] sulfur and salt, burned over — it is not sown and does not sprout and no grass grows in it — like the overthrow of Sedom and 'Amorah, Admah and Tsevoyim, which the LORD overthrew in his anger and in his wrath —

**24** then all the nations shall say: Why has the LORD done thus to this land? What is the greatness of this burning anger?

**25** Then they shall say: Because they forsook the covenant of the LORD, the God of their fathers, which he made with them when he brought them out from the land of Mitsrayim,

**26** and they went and served other gods and bowed down to them — gods whom they had not known and whom he had not allotted to them.

**27** And the anger of the LORD burned against that land to bring upon it all the curse [alah] written in this scroll.

**28** And the LORD uprooted them from upon their land in anger and in wrath and in great fury, and cast them into another land, as it is this day.

**29** The hidden things belong to the LORD our God [ha-nistarot la-YHWH Eloheinu], but the revealed things [belong] to us and to our sons forever — to do all the words of this Torah.

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

**V.1 — Hebrew chapter marker:** The MT (and LXX) places what English Bibles call 28:69 as 29:1. EMET follows the Hebrew numbering. The verse functions as a superscription/colophon to the Moab covenant, distinct from but complementary to the Horev covenant.

**V.4 — "Heart to understand":** Hebrew lev lada'at — "a heart to know." The theological claim is that divine cognition requires divine gift. Paul alludes to this in Rom 11:8 ("a spirit of stupor"). LXX: kardia eidenai — exact.

**V.9 — "Act wisely" (taskilu):** Hebrew tashkilu — from the root sakal, meaning to act wisely/prudently/successfully. LXX: συνησετε — "you shall understand/succeed."

**V.14–15 — Covenant with the absent:** The covenant extends to those "not here with us today" — interpreted in rabbinic tradition as including future generations and converts. LXX: τοις παρεστηκοσιν ωδε σημερον ("those standing here today") versus τοις μη παρεστηκοσιν ("those not standing here today") — confirms the MT reading.

**V.18 — "Root bearing poisonous and bitter fruit" (rosh ve-la'anah):** Rosh is often translated "hemlock" or "wormwood/poison." EMET renders literally with the paired idiom. LXX: ρίζα ανω φύουσα εν χολή και πικρία — "a root growing upward in gall and bitterness." The image recurs in Hebrews 12:15.

**V.19 — "To add the watered to the thirsty":** A cryptic idiom (lesafot ha-ravah et ha-tseme'ah) — meaning approximately "so that the satisfied person brings ruin along with the unsatisfied," i.e., the self-deceived person drags others into destruction. LXX: ινα μη συναπολεση ο αμαρτωλος τον αναμαρτητον — "lest the sinner destroy the innocent alongside himself" — an interpretive expansion rather than literal rendering.

**V.23 — Sedom, 'Amorah, Admah, Tsevoyim:** The four cities of the plain. LXX preserves all four: Σοδομα, Γομορρα, Αδαμα, Σεβωιμ. The comparison of the desolated land to the plain of Sedom is one of the most vivid desolation images in the Torah.

**V.29 — Ha-nistarot la-YHWH Eloheinu:** "The hidden things belong to the LORD our God." The verse has a unique scribal tradition: in many MSS the letters of nistarot ("hidden things") and lanu ("to us") are written with raised dots (puncta extraordinaria) above them — indicating textual uncertainty or special marking. EMET notes this without altering the text. LXX: τα κρυπτα κυριω τω θεω ημων — "the hidden things [belong] to the LORD our God." The final verse of the chapter stands as the epistemological principle of the whole covenant: some things are beyond human knowing.

— EMET
